g.data-narration: In this topic, we will learn how to compute characteristic and minimal polynomials for various types of block matrices including diagonal, triangular, and Kronecker product forms.
g.text: Characteristic and Minimal Polynomials of Block Matrices

g.data-narration: A block matrix is partitioned into submatrices by horizontal and vertical lines. For example, a 2x2 block matrix contains four blocks A11, A12, A21, and A22.
g.text: A block matrix is partitioned into submatrices by horizontal and vertical lines.
A = [A11 A12] where each Aij is a submatrix (block)

g.data-narration: For block diagonal matrices, the characteristic polynomial equals the product of characteristic polynomials of each diagonal block. This simplifies computations significantly.
For block triangular matrices, eigenvalues are the union of diagonal block eigenvalues. The characteristic polynomial is still the product of diagonal blocks.
g.text: For block upper triangular A = [A11 A12; 0 A22]:
Eigenvalues = union of eigenvalues from all diagonal blocks

g.data-narration: The minimal polynomial of block diagonal matrices is the least common multiple of minimal polynomials of individual blocks, not their product.
The Schur complement provides a formula for determinants of block matrices. When A11 is invertible, det(A) equals det(A11) times det of the Schur complement.
g.text: For A = [A11 A12; A21 A22] with invertible A11:
Used to compute determinants without expanding the full matrix

g.data-narration: For Kronecker products, the characteristic polynomial follows a special formula: p_A⊗B equals p_B raised to power m times p_A raised to power n, where A is m-by-m and B is n-by-n.
g.text: Eigenvalues of A⊗B = products of eigenvalues of A and B
Tip: Remember LCM for minimal polynomial, PRODUCT for characteristic polynomial!
Cayley-Hamilton: pA(A)=0 always | Minimal polynomial degree = largest Jordan block size
